en.extract:
The wattled brushturkey (Aepypodius arfakianus) is a species of bird in the family Megapodiidae found year-round in New Guinea. Its natural habitats are subtropical or tropical moist lowland forest and subtropical or tropical moist montane forest.


Taxonomy
The wattled brush-turkey was first formally described from collected chick specimens by Italian ornithologist Tommaso Salvadori in 1877 under the name Talegalla arfakianus. In 1881, French zoologist Émile Oustalet first described its adult specimens and named them as Talegallus (Aepypodius) pyrrhopygius, unaware that they represented the same species. The difference in plumage between the young and adults led to the temporary separation until 1893, when a comparison of specimens confirmed that both names referred to the same taxon. Following this revision, the species was placed in the genus Aepypodius as Aepypodius arfakianus.
The wattled brush-turkey belongs to the Megapode family (Megapodiidae) within the order Galliformes. It is closely related to the Waigeo brushturkey and can be told apart from other brushturkeys of the Talegalla genus by its lack of wattles on the side of the neck.
The genus name is derived from the Greek words αἰπύς (aipus), meaning "steep" or "high," and ποδός (podos), meaning "foot," alluding to its strong legs and preference for highly elevated habitats. The specific epithet arfakianus refers to the Arfak Mountains of northwestern New Guinea, where the species was first described.


Subspecies
There are two recognized subspecies:


Description
The wattled brushturkey is a relatively large megapode, with adults measuring about 38-46 cm in length and weighing roughly between 1.2 and 1.6 kg. Its plumage is predominantly black, with the face dull and mostly bare, but it is easily identified by its deep chestnut rump and feathered nape. It also possesses proportionally larger tarsi than other megapodes, with colour ranging from grey to brown. The species shows sexual dimorphism, with males being slightly larger and bearing more developed head ornamentation than females.

Adult males are distinguished by their brightly coloured chicken-like combs on the forehead and their single pendulous wattles hanging from the throat. Both structures enlarge during periods of sexual activity, with the comb reaching up to 3 cm in height and the wattle extending to roughly 6 cm. Ornament colouration also differs greatly between subspecies. In the nominate subspecies, the comb and lower wattle usually display bright red hues, with the remaining bare skin exhibiting pale bluish tones. In contrast, the Misool subspecies is known to display striking light blue combs.
Adult females tend to resemble males but are noticeably smaller and less ornamented than them. The head is more densely covered in short bristle feathers, and the fleshy comb is reduced to a low, narrow ridge. The single wattle is present but thin and short, lacking the dramatic enlargement seen in males during the breeding period. The bare head and neck are also more subdued in colour. The length of the feet tends to be smaller, though they maintain their characteristic robustness.
Newly hatched chicks are highly precocial, emerging from the mound fully feathered. The body is predominantly brown, with an ochre feathered head and dark wings reaching a sooty black colour.


Distribution and habitat


Distribution
The wattled brushturkey is found in the montane regions of New Guinea, where its nominate subspecies contains a broad but fragmented range of approximately 685,000 km2. It occurs along the central mountain chain from the east Sudirman Range to the west Owen Stanley Range and in several other elevated areas, such as the Tamrau and Arfak Mountains of the Bird's Head Peninsula. The second subspecies is restricted to Misool Island in the Raja Ampat archipelago, focused in the central limestone hills. A small population has also been observed on Yapen, where nesting mounds have been recorded at 1,250 m elevation, though its subspecies remains unknown.


Habitat
The wattled brushturkey is known to favour steep forested mountainsides with a strong reliance on dense forest cover and can be found at elevations above 1,000 m. In areas where it overlaps with Talegalla and Megapodius species, the wattled brushturkey occurs mainly within the 750-1,500 m elevation band.


Behaviour
The wattled brushturkey is a shy and inconspicuous bird, spending most of its time on the ground. When disturbed, the species flies up into trees, where it roosts until the perceived danger is gone. Adults are generally solitary and do not form cohesive flocks, while immature birds are speculated to live in small groups.


Diet
Like other megapodes, the species is an opportunistic ground forager. It scratches through leaf litter in search of fallen fruits, seeds, insects, and other invertebrates, therefore making it omnivorous. They tend to forage during the day, from early in the morning to late afternoon, a pattern that is shared with other megapodes.


Vocalization
The wattled brushturkey has a varied vocal repertoire. Its primary call is described as a harsh crowing of six down-slurred notes, transcribed as "kyew kyew-kyew-kyew-kyew kyew". At the start of the breeding season, males give a distinctive rhythmic call, consisting of about seven repeated notes, transcribed as "hja hja hjahjahjahja hja," that lasts for roughly 3 seconds. As the season progresses, the call becomes shorter and less rhythmic, decreasing to five notes. By the end of the season, males give only two or one note, lasting for about 1.3 seconds. 
Females, on the other hand, only give a brief, single-syllable call while laying eggs at the mound. No contact calls between the sexes have been reported. 


Breeding
As is typical of megapodes, wattled brushturkeys do not brood their eggs. Instead, they build and maintain large mounds, where eggs are buried and incubated without the presence of the adults. The mounds are built by the male over several weeks and consist of amalgamated leaf litter, soil, and other plant material. Heat is produced inside the mound as it naturally decomposes, with the male consistently regulating the internal temperature by adding or removing material as needed. Once the eggs are laid, they incubate for 50 to 70 days. Chicks hatch underground and make their way to the surface by digging upward with their feet. Once they emerge, they are fully feathered and capable of flight. They leave the mound immediately without ever receiving parental care.


Conservation status
Although its population trend is decreasing, the wattled brushturkey is currently classified as Least Concern on the IUCN Red List as of 2024. However, the isolated Misool subspecies is regarded as Vulnerable due to its very limited distribution range and population size. The subspecies is threatened by habitat loss due to the logging of the forested limestone hills where it is known to reside.


References

The '''wattled brushturkey''' ('''''Aepypodius arfakianus''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Megapodiidae]] found year-round in [[New Guinea]]. Its natural [[habitat]]s are [[subtropical or tropical moist lowland forest]] and subtropical or tropical moist [[montane forest]].<ref name="iucn status 12 November 2021" />

== Taxonomy ==
The wattled brush-turkey was first [[Species description|formally described]] from collected chick specimens by Italian ornithologist [[Tommaso Salvadori]] in 1877 under the name ''Talegalla arfakianus''.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last=Salvadori |first=Tommaso |author-link=Tommaso Salvadori |orig-date=1876-1877 |editor-last=Doria |editor-first=Giacomo |editor-link=Giacomo Doria |editor2-last=Gestro |editor2-first=Raffaello |editor2-link=Raffaello Gestro |title=Intorno alle specie del genere Talegallus |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/item/94504#page/327/mode/1up |journal=Annali del Museo Civico di Storia Naturale di Genova |language=it |location=Genova |publisher=Tipografia del R. Istituto Sordo-Muti |volume=9 |pages=332-333}}</ref> In 1881, French zoologist [[Émile Oustalet]] first described its adult specimens and named them as ''Talegallus'' (''Aepypodius'') ''pyrrhopygius'', unaware that they represented the same species.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Oustalet |first=Émile |author-link=Émile Oustalet |orig-date=1881 |editor-last=Milne-Edwards |editor-first=Henri |editor-link=Henri Milne-Edwards |editor2-last=Milne-Edwards |editor2-first=Alphonse |editor2-link=Alphonse Milne-Edwards |title=Monographie des oiseaux de la famille des Mégapodiidés. |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/bibliography/4647 |journal=Annales des Sciences Naturelles: Zoologie et Paléontologie |series=6 |language=fr |publisher=G. Masson |publication-place=Paris |volume=11 |pages=27, 40-43}}</ref> The difference in plumage between the young and adults led to the temporary separation until 1893, when a comparison of specimens confirmed that both names referred to the same [[taxon]]. Following this revision, the species was placed in the genus ''Aepypodius'' as ''Aepypodius arfakianus''.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Ogilvie-Grant |first=William Robert |author-link=William Robert Ogilvie-Grant |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/bibliography/8233 |title=Catalogue of the Birds in the British Museum |publisher=Order of the Trustees |year=1893 |volume=22 |location=London |pages=469-471 |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite book |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/item/40866 |title=Notes from the Leyden Museum |publisher=E. J. Brill |year=1908 |editor-last=Schlegel |editor-first=Hermann |editor-link=Hermann Schlegel |volume=29}}</ref>

The wattled brush-turkey belongs to the Megapode family (Megapodiidae) within the order [[Galliformes]].<ref name=":2" /> It is closely related to the [[Waigeo brushturkey]] and can be told apart from other brushturkeys of the ''[[Talegalla]]'' genus by its lack of wattles on the side of the neck.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":3">{{Cite book |last=Jones |first=Darryl N. |title=The megapodes: Megapodiidae |last2=Dekker |first2=René W. R. J. |last3=Roselaar |first3=Cees S. |date=1995 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-854651-1 |series=Bird families of the world |location=Oxford [England] ; New York}}</ref>

The genus name is derived from the Greek words αἰπύς (''aipus''), meaning "steep" or "high," and ποδός (''podos''), meaning "foot," alluding to its strong legs and preference for highly elevated habitats.<ref name=":3" /> The [[specific epithet]] ''arfakianus'' refers to the [[Arfak Mountains]] of northwestern New Guinea, where the species was first described.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":3" />

=== Subspecies ===
There are two recognized subspecies:

==Description==
The wattled brushturkey is a relatively large [[megapode]], with adults measuring about 38-46 cm in length and weighing roughly between 1.2 and 1.6 kg.<ref name="oiseaux2">{{cite web |title=''Wattled Brushturkey Aepypodius arfakianus - Talégalle des Arfak'' |url=https://www.oiseaux.net/birds/wattled.brushturkey.html}}</ref> Its plumage is predominantly black, with the face dull and mostly bare, but it is easily identified by its deep chestnut rump and feathered nape.<ref>{{Cite book |title=Handbook of the Birds of the World |date=1995 |publisher=Lynx Edicions |isbn=978-84-87334-10-8 |editor-last=del Hoyo |editor-first=Josep |editor-link=Josep del Hoyo |volume=2 |location=Barcelona |editor-last2=Elliott |editor-first2=Andrew |editor-link2=Andrew Elliott (ornithologist) |editor-last3=Sargatal |editor-first3=Jordi |editor-link3=Jordi Sargatal}}</ref> It also possesses proportionally larger [[Tarsus (skeleton)|tarsi]] than other megapodes, with colour ranging from grey to brown. The species shows [[Sexual dimorphism in birds|sexual dimorphism]], with males being slightly larger and bearing more developed head ornamentation than females.<ref name="oiseaux2" />
[[File:Wattled Brushturkey features.png|thumb|241x241px|Key identification features]]Adult males are distinguished by their brightly coloured chicken-like [[Comb (anatomy)|combs]] on the forehead and their single pendulous [[Wattle (anatomy)|wattles]] hanging from the throat. Both structures enlarge during periods of sexual activity, with the comb reaching up to 3 cm in height and the wattle extending to roughly 6 cm. Ornament colouration also differs greatly between subspecies. In the nominate subspecies, the comb and lower wattle usually display bright red hues, with the remaining bare skin exhibiting pale bluish tones. In contrast, the Misool subspecies is known to display striking light blue combs.<ref name="oiseaux2" />

Adult females tend to resemble males but are noticeably smaller and less ornamented than them. The head is more densely covered in short bristle feathers, and the fleshy comb is reduced to a low, narrow ridge. The single wattle is present but thin and short, lacking the dramatic enlargement seen in males during the breeding period. The bare head and neck are also more subdued in colour. The length of the feet tends to be smaller, though they maintain their characteristic robustness.<ref name="oiseaux2" />

Newly hatched chicks are highly [[Precociality and altriciality|precocial]], emerging from the mound fully feathered. The body is predominantly brown, with an ochre feathered head and dark wings reaching a sooty black colour.<ref name="oiseaux2" />

== Distribution and habitat ==

=== Distribution ===
The wattled brushturkey is found in the montane regions of [[New Guinea]], where its nominate subspecies contains a broad but fragmented range of approximately 685,000 km<sup>2</sup>. It occurs along the central mountain chain from the east [[Sudirman Range]] to the west [[Owen Stanley Range]] and in several other elevated areas, such as the [[Tamrau Mountains|Tamrau]] and [[Arfak Mountains]] of the [[Bird's Head Peninsula]]. The second subspecies is restricted to [[Misool|Misool Island]] in the [[Raja Ampat Islands|Raja Ampat]] archipelago, focused in the central limestone hills. A small population has also been observed on [[Yapen]], where nesting mounds have been recorded at 1,250 m elevation, though its subspecies remains unknown.<ref name=":3" /><ref name="iucn status 12 November 2021" />

=== Habitat ===
The wattled brushturkey is known to favour steep forested mountainsides with a strong reliance on dense forest cover and can be found at [[Elevation|elevations]] above 1,000 m. In areas where it overlaps with ''[[Talegalla]]'' and ''[[Scrubfowl|Megapodius]]'' species, the wattled brushturkey occurs mainly within the 750-1,500 m elevation band.<ref name=":3" />

== Behaviour ==
The wattled brushturkey is a shy and inconspicuous bird, spending most of its time on the ground. When disturbed, the species flies up into trees, where it roosts until the perceived danger is gone. Adults are generally solitary and do not form cohesive flocks, while immature birds are speculated to live in small groups.<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":4">{{Cite journal|title=Wattled Brushturkey hunting system in the Sigim and Sinaitousi communities in the buffer zone of the Arfak Mountain Nature Reserve, West Papua, Indonesia|url=https://smujo.id/biodiv/article/view/2691|journal=Biodiversitas Journal of Biological Diversity|date=2018-05-01|issn=2085-4722|pages=849–856|volume=19|issue=3|doi=10.13057/biodiv/d190313|first=Hotlan|last=Manik|first2=Husmy|last2=Yurmiaty|first3=Indrawati Yudha|last3=Asmara|first4=Handarto|last4=Handarto|first5=Johan|last5=Iskandar|first6=Ruhyat|last6=Partasasmita|doi-access=free}}</ref>

=== Diet ===
Like other megapodes, the species is an opportunistic ground [[forager]]. It scratches through leaf litter in search of fallen fruits, seeds, [[Insect|insects]], and other [[Invertebrate|invertebrates]], therefore making it [[Omnivore|omnivorous]]. They tend to forage during the day, from early in the morning to late afternoon, a pattern that is shared with other megapodes.<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":4" /><ref name="ebird">{{cite web |title=''Wattled Brushturkey'' |url=https://ebird.org/species/watbrt1}}</ref>
{{Birdsong|species = ''Wattled Brushturkey''|url = https://xeno-canto.org/species/Aepypodius-arfakianus}}
=== Vocalization ===
The wattled brushturkey has a varied vocal repertoire. Its primary [[Bird call|call]] is described as a harsh crowing of six down-slurred notes, transcribed as "kyew kyew-kyew-kyew-kyew kyew". At the start of the breeding season, males give a distinctive rhythmic call, consisting of about seven repeated notes, transcribed as "hja hja hjahjahjahja hja," that lasts for roughly 3 seconds. As the season progresses, the call becomes shorter and less rhythmic, decreasing to five notes. By the end of the season, males give only two or one note, lasting for about 1.3 seconds.<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":5">{{Cite journal|title=Fortpflanzungsverhalten des Kamm-Talegalla (Aepypodius arfakianus Salvad.)|url=http://link.springer.com/10.1007/BF01647288|journal=Journal für Ornithologie|date=1988|issn=0021-8375|pages=185–204|volume=129|issue=2|doi=10.1007/BF01647288|language=de|first=Carola|last=Kloska|first2=Jürgen|last2=Nicolai|url-access=subscription}}</ref> 

Females, on the other hand, only give a brief, single-syllable call while laying eggs at the mound. No contact calls between the sexes have been reported.<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":5" /> 

=== Breeding ===
As is typical of megapodes, wattled brushturkeys do not [[Broodiness|brood]] their eggs. Instead, they build and maintain large mounds, where eggs are buried and [[Egg incubation|incubated]] without the presence of the adults. The mounds are built by the male over several weeks and consist of amalgamated leaf litter, soil, and other plant material. Heat is produced inside the mound as it naturally [[Decomposition|decomposes]], with the male consistently regulating the internal temperature by adding or removing material as needed. Once the eggs are laid, they incubate for 50 to 70 days. Chicks hatch underground and make their way to the surface by digging upward with their feet. Once they emerge, they are fully feathered and capable of flight. They leave the mound immediately without ever receiving [[Parental care in birds|parental care]].<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":5" />

== Conservation status ==
Although its population trend is decreasing, the wattled brushturkey is currently classified as [[Least Concern (IUCN Red List of Ecosystems)|Least Concern]] on the [[IUCN Red List]] as of 2024.<ref name="iucn status 12 November 2021" /> However, the isolated Misool subspecies is regarded as [[Vulnerable (IUCN Red List of Ecosystems)|Vulnerable]] due to its very limited distribution range and population size. The subspecies is threatened by habitat loss due to the [[logging]] of the forested limestone hills where it is known to reside.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Megapodes: an action plan for their conservation, 1995-1999|publisher=IUCN|date=1995|location=Gland|isbn=978-2-8317-0223-0|series=IUCN-SSC Action plans for the conservation of biological diversity|others=WPA/Birdlife/SSC Megapode specialist group|first=Renatus Wilhlemus Rembertus Jozef|last=Dekker|first2=Philip J. K.|last2=McGowan}}</ref>

zh.extract:
肉垂塚雉（学名：Aepypodius arfakianus），是鸡形目塚雉科的一种鸟类。
肉垂塚雉体重约1463.8克，翼长约266.8毫米，嘴峰长约35.4毫米，喙宽度约12毫米，喙厚度约17.6毫米，跗蹠长约83.4毫米，尾长约127.2毫米。它以植物的果实和种子为食，可能也会吃昆虫，并吞食石子以帮助消化食物。
肉垂塚雉在其分布区内为留鸟。它在全年的大多数时间内均可繁殖，巢主要用植物的枝叶建成，约300-350厘米宽，150-200厘米高，巢可能被暗色冢雉巢寄生。


亚种
A. a. arfakianus：分布于新几内亚和亚彭岛的高山。
A. a. misoliensis：分布于米苏尔岛的山峰环境。


保护
该物种属于无危级别，总种群数量可能在10,000到1,000,000之间，且较为稳定，未发现明显的威胁。但其亚种A. a. misoliensis受到栖息地破坏的影响；目前种群数量可能在100-10,000只之间，仍需进行更广泛的调查和研究。
